L1338: #  Method: projects.serviceAccounts.generateIdToken  Stay organized with collections  Save and categorize content based on your preferences.
L1339: 
L1340:   * cite937†HTTP request L1341:   * cite938†Path parameters L1342:   * cite939†Request body L1343:     * cite940†JSON representation L1344:   * cite941†Response body L1345:     * cite942†JSON representation L1346:   * cite943†Authorization scopes L1347:   * cite944†Try it! L1348: 
L1349: Generates an OpenID Connect ID token for a service account.
L1350: ### HTTP request
L1351: 
L1352: `POST https://iamcredentials.googleapis.com/v1/{name=projects/*/serviceAccounts/*}:generateIdToken `
L1353: 
L1354: The URL uses cite945†gRPC Transcoding†google.aip.dev syntax.
L1355: ### Path parameters
L1356: 
L1357:   Parameters
L1358:  `name`
L1359: 
L1360: `string`
L1361: 
L1362: Required. The resource name of the service account for which the credentials are requested, in the following format: `projects/-/serviceAccounts/{ACCOUNT_EMAIL_OR_UNIQUEID}`. The `-` wildcard character is required; replacing it with a project ID is invalid.
L1363: 
L1364: Authorization requires the following cite946†IAM†cloud.google.com permission on the specified resource `name`:
L1365: 
L1366:   * `iam.serviceAccounts.getOpenIdToken`
L1367: ### Request body
L1368: 
L1369: The request body contains data with the following structure:
L1370: 
L1371:   JSON representation
L1372: 
L1373:     {
L1374:       "delegates": [
L1375:         string
L1376:       ],
L1377:       "audience": string,
L1378:       "includeEmail": boolean,
L1379:       "organizationNumberIncluded": boolean
L1380:     }
L1381: 
L1382:    Fields
L1383:  `delegates[]`
L1384: 
L1385: `string`
L1386: The sequence of service accounts in a delegation chain. Each service account must be granted the `roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator` role on its next service account in the chain. The last service account in the chain must be granted the `roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator` role on the service account that is specified in the `name` field of the request.
L1387: The delegates must have the following format: `projects/-/serviceAccounts/{ACCOUNT_EMAIL_OR_UNIQUEID}`. The `-` wildcard character is required; replacing it with a project ID is invalid.
L1388: 
L1389:  `audience`
L1390: 
L1391: `string`
L1392: 
L1393: Required. The audience for the token, such as the API or account that this token grants access to.
L1394: 
L1395:  `includeEmail`
L1396: 
L1397: `boolean`
L1398: 
L1399: Include the service account email in the token. If set to `true`, the token will contain `email` and `email_verified` claims.
L1400: 
L1401:  `organizationNumberIncluded`
L1402: `boolean`
L1403: 
L1404: Include the organization number of the service account in the token. If set to `true`, the token will contain a `google.organization_number` claim. The value of the claim will be `null` if the service account isn't associated with an organization.
L1405: ### Response body
L1406: 
L1407: If successful, the response body contains data with the following structure:
L1408: 
L1409:   JSON representation
L1410: 
L1411:     {
L1412:       "token": string
L1413:     }
L1414: 
L1415:    Fields
L1416:  `token`
L1417: 
L1418: `string`
L1419: 
L1420: The OpenId Connect ID token.
L1421: 
L1422: The token is a JSON Web Token (JWT) that contains a payload with claims. See the cite947†JSON Web Token spec†tools.ietf.org for more information. Here is an example of a decoded JWT payload:
L1423:     {
L1424:       "iss": "https://accounts.google.com",
L1425:       "iat": 1496953245,
L1426:       "exp": 1496953245,
L1427:       "aud": "https://www.example.com",
L1428:       "sub": "107517467455664443765",
L1429:       "azp": "107517467455664443765",
L1430:       "email": "my-iam-account@my-project.iam.gserviceaccount.com",
L1431:       "email_verified": true,
L1432:       "google": {
L1433:         "organization_number": 123456
L1434:       }
L1435:     }
L1436: ### Authorization scopes
L1437: 
L1438: Requires one of the following OAuth scopes:
L1439: 
L1440:   * `https://www.googleapis.com/auth/iam`
L1441:   * `https://www.googleapis.com/auth/cloud-platform`
L1442: 
L1443: For more information, see the cite948†Authentication Overview .

L392: `email` The user's email address. Provided only if you included the `email` scope in your request. The value of this claim may not be unique to this account and could change over time, therefore you shouldn't use this value as the primary identifier to link to your user record. You also can't rely on the domain of the `email` claim to identify users of Google Workspace or Cloud organizations; use the `hd` claim instead. Warning: Don't use the `email` field as a unique identifier for a user.
L397:  `hd` The domain associated with the Google Workspace or Cloud organization of the user. Provided only if the user belongs to a Google Cloud organization. You must check this claim when restricting access to a resource to only members of certain domains. The absence of this claim indicates that the account does not belong to a Google hosted domain.
L398: `locale` The user's locale, represented by a cite84†BCP 47†tools.ietf.org language tag. Might be provided when a cite83†name claim is present.
L400:   * The request scope included the string "profile"
L401:   * The ID token is returned from a token refresh
L402: 
L403: When `name` claims are present, you can use them to update your app's user records. Note that this claim is never guaranteed to be present.
L404: 
L405:  `nonce` The value of the `nonce` supplied by your app in the authentication request. You should protect against replay attacks by presenting this value only once.
L406: `picture` The URL of the user's profile picture. Might be provided when:
L407:   * The request scope included the string "profile"
L408:   * The ID token is returned from a token refresh
L409: 
L410: When `picture` claims are present, you can use them to update your app's user records. Note that this claim is never guaranteed to be present.
L411: 
L412: `profile` The URL of the user's profile page. Might be provided when:
L413: 
L414:   * The request scope included the string "profile"
L415:   * The ID token is returned from a token refresh
L417: #### 6. Authenticate the user
L418: 
L419: After obtaining user information from the ID token, you should query your app's user database. If the user already exists in your database, you should start an application session for that user if all login requirements are met by the Google API response.
L420: If the user does not exist in your user database, you should redirect the user to your new-user sign-up flow. You may be able to auto-register the user based on the information you receive from Google, or at the very least you may be able to pre-populate many of the fields that you require on your registration form. In addition to the information in the ID token, you can get additional cite85†user profile information at our user profile endpoints.
L425: One of the advantages of using OAuth 2.0 for authentication is that your application can get permission to use other Google APIs on behalf of the user (such as YouTube, Google Drive, Calendar, or Contacts) at the same time as you authenticate the user. To do this, include the other scopes that you need in the cite50†authentication request that you send to Google.
L426: For example, to add user's age group to your authentication request, pass a scope parameter of `openid email https://www.googleapis.com/auth/profile.agerange.read`. The user is prompted appropriately on the cite86†consent screen . The access token that you receive back from Google will let your application access all the APIs related to the scopes of access you requested and were granted.
L446:  `response_type` (Required) If the value is `code`, launches a cite89†Basic authorization code flow†openid.net , requiring a `POST` to the token endpoint to obtain the tokens. If the value is `token id_token` or `id_token token`, launches an cite90†Implicit flow†openid.net , requiring the use of JavaScript at the redirect URI to retrieve tokens from the cite91†URI #fragment identifier†datatracker.ietf.org .
L447:  `redirect_uri` (Required) Determines where the response is sent. The value of this parameter must exactly match one of the authorized redirect values that you set in the Cloud Console cite49†Clients page†console.developers.google.com (including the HTTP or HTTPS scheme, case, and trailing '/', if any).
L448:  `scope` (Required)
L449: The scope parameter must begin with the `openid` value and then include the `profile` value, the `email` value, or both.
L450: 
L451: If the `profile` scope value is present, the ID token might (but is not guaranteed to) include the user's default `profile` claims.
L452: 
L453: If the `email` scope value is present, the ID token includes `email` and `email_verified` claims.
L454: In addition to these OpenID-specific scopes, your scope argument can also include other scope values. All scope values must be space-separated. For example, if you wanted per-file access to a user's Google Drive, your scope parameter might be `openid profile email https://www.googleapis.com/auth/drive.file`.
L455: 
L456: For information about available scopes, see cite92†OAuth 2.0 Scopes for Google APIs or the documentation for the Google API you would like to use.
L457: 
L458:  `state` (Optional, but strongly recommended)
L459: An opaque string that is round-tripped in the protocol; that is to say, it is returned as a URI parameter in the Basic flow, and in the URI `#fragment` identifier in the Implicit flow.
L460: The `state` can be useful for correlating requests and responses. Because your `redirect_uri` can be guessed, using a `state` value can increase your assurance that an incoming connection is the result of an authentication request initiated by your app. If you cite56†generate a random string or encode the hash of some client state (e.g., a cookie) in this `state` variable, you can validate the response to verify that the request and response originated in the same browser.
L463:  `claims` (Optional) The cite93† claims parameter†openid.net is used to specify one or more optional fields to include in the userinfo endpoint or authentication request ID token response types. The value is a JSON object containing the response type and requested claims. The following claim requests are accepted by Google servers:  Claim requests
L497: One thing that makes ID tokens useful is that fact that you can pass them around different components of your app. These components can use an ID token as a lightweight authentication mechanism authenticating the app and the user. But before you can use the information in the ID token or rely on it as an assertion that the user has authenticated, you must validate it.
L498: 
L499: Validation of an ID token requires several steps:
L500:   1. Verify that the ID token is properly signed by the issuer. Google-issued tokens are signed using one of the certificates found at the URI specified in the `jwks_uri` metadata value of the cite65†Discovery document .
L501:   2. Verify that the value of the `iss` claim in the ID token is equal to `https://accounts.google.com` or `accounts.google.com`.
L502:   3. Verify that the value of the `aud` claim in the ID token is equal to your app's client ID.
L503:   4. Verify that the expiry time (`exp` claim) of the ID token has not passed.
L504:   5. If you specified a hd parameter value in the request, verify that the ID token has a `hd` claim that matches an accepted domain associated with a Google Cloud organization.
L505: Steps 2 to 5 involve only string and date comparisons which are quite straightforward, so we won't detail them here.
L506: The first step is more complex, and involves cryptographic signature checking. For debugging purposes, you can use Google's `tokeninfo` endpoint to compare against local processing implemented on your server or device. Suppose your ID token's value is `XYZ123`. Then you would dereference the URI `https://oauth2.googleapis.com/tokeninfo?id_token=XYZ123`. If the token signature is valid, the response would be the JWT payload in its decoded JSON object form.
L507: The `tokeninfo` endpoint is useful for debugging but for production purposes, retrieve Google's public keys from the keys endpoint and perform the validation locally. You should retrieve the keys URI from the cite65†Discovery document using the `jwks_uri` metadata value. Requests to the debugging endpoint may be throttled or otherwise subject to intermittent errors.
L508: Since Google changes its public keys only infrequently, you can cache them using the cache directives of the HTTP response and, in the vast majority of cases, perform local validation much more efficiently than by using the `tokeninfo` endpoint. This validation requires retrieving and parsing certificates, and making the appropriate cryptographic calls to check the signature.
L509: Fortunately, there are well-debugged libraries available in a wide variety of languages to accomplish this (see cite96†jwt.io†jwt.io ).
L510: ### Obtaining user profile information
L511: 
L512: To obtain additional profile information about the user, you can use the access token (which your application receives during the cite97†authentication flow ) and the cite98†OpenID Connect†openid.net standard:
L513: 
L514:   1. To be OpenID-compliant, you must include the cite99†openid profile scope values in your cite50†authentication request .
L515: If you want the user's email address to be included, you can specify an additional scope value of cite100†email . To specify both `profile` and `email`, you can include the following parameter in your authentication request URI:
L516: 
L517:         scope=openid%20profile%20email
L518:   2. Add your access token to the authorization header and make an HTTPS `GET` request to the userinfo endpoint, which you should retrieve from the cite65†Discovery document using the `userinfo_endpoint` metadata value. The userinfo response includes information about the user, as described in cite101†OpenID Connect Standard Claims†openid.net and the `claims_supported` metadata value of the Discovery document.
L519: Users or their organizations may choose to supply or withhold certain fields, so you might not get information for every field for your authorized scopes of access.
L520: ## The Discovery document
L521: 
L522: The OpenID Connect protocol requires the use of multiple endpoints for authenticating users, and for requesting resources including tokens, user information, and public keys.
L523: To simplify implementations and increase flexibility, OpenID Connect allows the use of a "Discovery document," a JSON document found at a well-known location containing key-value pairs which provide details about the OpenID Connect provider's configuration, including the URIs of the authorization, token, revocation, userinfo, and public-keys endpoints. The Discovery document for Google's OpenID Connect service may be retrieved from:
L524: 
L525:     https://accounts.google.com/.well-known/openid-configuration
L526: To use Google's OpenID Connect services, you should hard-code the Discovery-document URI (`https://accounts.google.com/.well-known/openid-configuration`) into your application. Your application fetches the document, applies caching rules in the response, then retrieves endpoint URIs from it as needed.
L527: For example, to authenticate a user, your code would retrieve the `authorization_endpoint` metadata value (`https://accounts.google.com/o/oauth2/v2/auth` in the example below) as the base URI for authentication requests that are sent to Google.
L528: Here is an example of such a document; the field names are those specified in cite102†OpenID Connect Discovery 1.0†openid.net (refer to that document for their meanings). The values are purely illustrative and might change, although they are copied from a recent version of the actual Google Discovery document:
L529:     {
L530:       "issuer": "https://accounts.google.com",
L531:       "authorization_endpoint": "https://accounts.google.com/o/oauth2/v2/auth",
L532:       "device_authorization_endpoint": "https://oauth2.googleapis.com/device/code",
L533:       "token_endpoint": "https://oauth2.googleapis.com/token",
L534:       "userinfo_endpoint": "https://openidconnect.googleapis.com/v1/userinfo",
L535:       "revocation_endpoint": "https://oauth2.googleapis.com/revoke",
L536:       "jwks_uri": "https://www.googleapis.com/oauth2/v3/certs",
L537:       "response_types_supported": [
L538:         "code",
L539:         "token",
L540:         "id_token",
L541:         "code token",
L542:         "code id_token",
L543:         "token id_token",
L544:         "code token id_token",
L545:         "none"
L546:       ],

L179: For information about ID token contents and lifetimes, see cite74†ID tokens .
L180: ID tokens have a specific service or application that they can be used for, specified by the value of their `aud` claim. This document uses the term target service to refer to the service or application that the ID token can be used to authenticate to.
L181: 
L182: When you get the ID token, you can include it in an `Authorization` header in the request to the target service.
L183: ## Methods for getting an ID token
L184: 
L185: There are various ways to get an ID token. This page describes the following methods:
L186: 
L187:   * cite75†Get an ID token from the metadata server L188:   * cite76†Use a connecting service to generate an ID token L189:   * cite77†Generate an ID token by impersonating a service account L190:   * cite78†Generate a generic ID token for development with Cloud Run and Cloud Run functions L191: If you need an ID token to be accepted by an application not hosted on Google Cloud, you can probably use these methods. However, you should determine what ID token claims the application requires.
L192: ### Get an ID token from the metadata server
L193: 
L194: When your code is running on a resource that can have a cite79†service account attached to it , the metadata server for the associated service can usually provide an ID token. The metadata server generates ID tokens for the attached service account. You cannot get an ID token based on user credentials from the metadata server.
L195: 
L196: You can get an ID token from the metadata server when your code is running on the following Google Cloud services:
L197:   * cite80† Compute Engine L198:   * cite81† App Engine standard environment L199:   * cite82† App Engine flexible environment L200:   * cite83† Cloud Run functions L201:   * cite84†Cloud Run L202:   * cite85† Google Kubernetes Engine L203:   * cite86† Cloud Build L204: 
L205: To retrieve an ID token from the metadata server, you query the identity endpoint for the service account, as shown in this example.
L206: ###  curl
L207: 
L208: Replace `AUDIENCE` with the URI for the target service, for example `http://www.example.com`.
L209: 
L210:     curl -H "Metadata-Flavor: Google" \
L211:       'http://metadata/computeMetadata/v1/instance/service-accounts/default/identity?audience=AUDIENCE'
L212: ###  PowerShell
L213: 
L214: Replace `AUDIENCE` with the URI for the target service, for example `http://www.example.com`.
L215:

L217:       -Headers @{'Metadata-Flavor' = 'Google'} `
L218:       -Uri "http://metadata/computeMetadata/v1/instance/service-accounts/default/identity?audience=AUDIENCE")
L219:     $value

L380: ### Use a connecting service to generate an ID token
L381: 
L382: Some Google Cloud services help you call other services. These connecting services might help determine when the call gets made, or manage a workflow that includes calling the service. The following services can automatically include an ID token, with the appropriate value for the `aud` claim, when they initiate a call to a service that requires an ID token:
L386:      Cloud Tasks lets you manage the execution of distributed tasks. You can configure a task to include either an ID token or an access token when it calls a service. For more information, see cite108† Using HTTP Target tasks with authentication tokens .
L387: Pub/Sub
L388:      Pub/Sub enables asynchronous communication between services. You can configure Pub/Sub to include an ID token with a message. For more information, see cite109† Authentication for push subscription .
L389: Workflows
L395: Service account impersonation should always involve two principals: a principal that represents the caller, and the service account that is being impersonated, called the privilege-bearing service account.
L396: 
L397: To generate an ID token by impersonating a service account, you use the following general process.
L398: 
L399: For step-by-step instructions, see cite111†Create an ID token .
L400: 
L401:   1. Identify or cite112†create a service account to be the privilege-bearing service account.
L402:   2. Identify the required roles to invoke the target service. Grant these roles to the service account on the target service:
L403: 
L404:      * For cite113† Cloud Run services , grant the Cloud Run Invoker role (`roles/run.invoker`).
L405:      * For cite71†Cloud Run functions , grant the Cloud Functions Invoker role (`roles/cloudfunctions.invoker`).
L406:      * For other target services, see the product documentation for the service.
L410:   4. Grant the principal the Service Account OpenID Connect Identity Token Creator role (`roles/iam.serviceAccountOpenIdTokenCreator`).
L411: 
L412:   5. Use the cite114†IAM Credentials API to generate the ID token for the authorized service account.
L413: 
L414: Replace the following:
L415: 
L416:      * AUDIENCE: The URI for the target service&mdashfor example, `http://www.example.com`.
L417:      * SERVICE_ACCOUNT_EMAIL: The email address of the privilege-bearing service account.

1	# cyspbot
     2	
     3	cyspbot is the maintainer's hosted automation application. It lets trusted automation workloads obtain repository-scoped GitHub App installation access tokens without exposing the GitHub App private key outside Cloudflare.
     4	
     5	The service contract is [docs/service-contract.md](docs/service-contract.md). The implementation reference is [docs/implementation.md](docs/implementation.md).
     6	
     7	## Language
     8	
     9	**Caller**:
    10	An automation workload that presents an OIDC token from a configured issuer to **cyspbot**.
    11	_Avoid_: User, human, consumer
    12	
    13	**Verified Subject Token**:
    14	The cyspbot-internal authentication result after an OIDC/JWT subject token has been cryptographically verified against a **Trusted OIDC Issuer**, checked for the cyspbot audience, and accepted by issuer-adapter token-binding checks.
    15	_Avoid_: Principal, raw JWT, unverified subject
    16	
    17	**Subject Token Claims**:
    18	The verified JWT claims carried by a **Verified Subject Token**. **Token Policy** may read these through CEL as `claims["..."]`, but cyspbot does not require issuer-specific claims unless a checked-in policy condition names them.
    19	_Avoid_: Derived principal fields, caller-provided attributes
    20	
    21	**Fly Machine Identity**:
    22	The internally consistent organization, app, and Machine identity authenticated from a Fly.io subject token. It requires immutable IDs, binds the organization name to the configured issuer slug, and binds the subject to the organization, app, and Machine names.
    23	_Avoid_: Fly user, app secret, unverified Machine metadata
    24	
    25	**Google Service Account Identity**:
    26	The immutable service-account unique ID authenticated when a Google Accounts ID token has matching non-empty `sub` and `azp` claims. Policy may additionally require a signed email only when `email_verified` is true.
    27	_Avoid_: Google user, unverified email, service-account key
    28	
    29	**Installation Token Issuance**:
    30	The cyspbot capability that exchanges a trusted OIDC token for a short-lived GitHub App installation access token for callers that satisfy cyspbot's checked-in OIDC trust policy.
    31	_Avoid_: cyspbot itself, app login
    32	
    33	**Installation Token Request**:
    34	The normalized cyspbot-internal request for one GitHub App installation access token. It contains exactly one canonical GitHub repository API resource and the GitHub App permissions requested for that resource.
    35	_Avoid_: Profile, grant, target selector, raw form values
    36	
    37	**Repository Resource**:
    38	A canonical GitHub API repository URI in the form `https://api.github.com/repos/{owner}/{repo}`. One **Installation Token Request** contains exactly one **Repository Resource**.
    39	_Avoid_: `owner/repo` shorthand, GitHub HTML URL, workflow endpoint URL
    40	
    41	**GitHub App Installation**:
    42	The installation of the configured GitHub App on a specific repository or owner scope for which GitHub can issue a GitHub App installation access token.
    43	_Avoid_: App session, app login
    44	
    45	**Installation Token**:
     1	# Security Policy
     2	
     3	## Reporting Vulnerabilities
     4	
     5	Please report security vulnerabilities privately through GitHub private vulnerability reporting when it is enabled for this repository.
     6	
     7	If private vulnerability reporting is unavailable, contact the repository maintainer without opening a public issue. Do not include exploit details, private keys, tokens, webhook secrets, session material, or tenant-specific deployment identifiers in public issues, pull requests, or discussions.
     8	
     9	## Security Boundary
    10	
    11	cyspbot exchanges verified OIDC tokens for repository-scoped GitHub App installation access tokens. The important security properties are:
    12	
    13	- issuer trust is configured, not discovered from caller-controlled tokens
    14	- authorization evaluates only claims from a token verified by a configured issuer adapter
    15	- Fly Machine authentication requires non-empty immutable identity claims and a canonical subject consistent with the configured organization, app name, and Machine name
    16	- Google service-account authentication requires non-empty matching `sub` and `azp` claims, and email-based policy constraints require `email_verified=true`
    17	- the token-exchange form audience selects one configured GitHub App, and the verified subject token audience must match it exactly
    18	- callers may request one canonical repository resource and an exact GitHub App permission scope
    19	- Token Policy must explicitly allow the normalized caller, GitHub App, resource, and permission combination before a token is issued
    20	- the GitHub App installation remains the upper-bound authority for repositories and permissions
    21	- the GitHub App private key remains inside the deployment secret boundary
    22	- webhook processing requires GitHub signature validation before state changes
    23	- webhook deliveries must identify the configured GitHub App before they are accepted
    24	
    25	## Deployment Secrets
    26	
    27	Never commit deployment secrets, local `.dev.vars`, `.env`, GitHub App private keys, webhook secrets, Cloudflare API tokens, or generated Wrangler state.
    28	
    29	The source repository intentionally carries only public-safe Wrangler templates for local development, tests, and dry-runs. Production deployment details, credentials, secret values, and deployment overlays must stay outside this codebase.

     1	# cyspbot
     2	
     3	cyspbot is a hosted Security Token Service for trusted automation workloads. It verifies OIDC tokens from configured GitHub Actions, Fly.io, and Google service-account issuers and exchanges allowed caller contexts for short-lived, repository-scoped GitHub App installation access tokens without exposing the GitHub App private key outside Cloudflare.
     4	
     5	Implemented public endpoints:
     6	
     7	- `POST /token` exchanges a trusted OIDC token for a scoped GitHub App installation access token.
     8	- `POST /github/webhooks` accepts signed GitHub App webhook deliveries and acknowledges them without retaining raw payloads or running downstream product logic.
     9	
    10	The primary service contract is [docs/service-contract.md](docs/service-contract.md). The implementation reference is [docs/implementation.md](docs/implementation.md). The documentation map is [docs/README.md](docs/README.md).
    11	
    12	## Implemented Architecture
    13	
    14	- Two deployable Worker packages under `workers/*`: `@cyspbot/token-exchange` and `@cyspbot/github-webhook-receiver`.
    15	- Worker names are consistently prefixed: `cyspbot-token-exchange` and `cyspbot-github-webhook-receiver`.
    16	- Each Worker package owns its runtime composition, HTTP route, dependency defaults, and Wrangler config. Shared implementation code lives under `packages/*`. The root Wrangler config is only the local/test binding harness.
    17	- `jose`-backed OIDC verification behind provider-owned issuer adapters for GitHub Actions, configured Fly.io organizations, and Google service accounts.
    18	- GitHub App private key in a Cloudflare Worker secret binding.
    19	- Checked-in Token Policy code that allows Installation Token Issuance only for explicit verified subject-token issuer, CEL claim condition, resource, and permission combinations.
    20	- A Fly Machine policy-rule builder that requires immutable organization and app IDs and can optionally narrow a grant to one immutable Machine ID.
    21	- A Google service-account policy-rule builder that requires the immutable account unique ID and can additionally require a verified email.
    22	
    23	## Current Public Surface
    24	
    25	### `POST /token`
    26	
    27	Primary endpoint for Installation Token Issuance. It accepts `application/x-www-form-urlencoded` OAuth token exchange input:
    28	
    29	```http
    30	grant_type=urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:token-exchange
    31	requested_token_type=urn:chikachow:github-app-installation-access-token
    32	subject_token=<github-actions-oidc-token>
    33	subject_token_type=urn:ietf:params:oauth:token-type:id_token
    34	```
    35	
    36	`subject_token_type` may also be `urn:ietf:params:oauth:token-type:jwt`. `requested_token_type` is required and must be the cyspbot GitHub App installation token URN.
    37	The subject token's verified OIDC `aud` claim must be the internal cyspbot service audience `cyspbot`. GitHub Actions and Fly.io accept an absent `azp` or require it to match `cyspbot`; Google service-account ID tokens require `azp` to equal the immutable service-account `sub`. cyspbot uses its service-owned GitHub App credential profile to resolve the target installation and evaluate Token Policy. Non-empty `audience` form parameters are rejected as unsupported target selectors.
    38	
    39	Requests may include RFC 8693 `scope` and `resource` fields to request a concrete GitHub App installation token shape. `resource` must be one canonical GitHub repository API URI in the form `https://api.github.com/repos/{owner}/{repo}` with no leading or trailing whitespace. `scope` is a single-ASCII-space-delimited list of exact GitHub App permission requests, such as `actions:read`, `actions:write`, or `contents:read pull_requests:read`; scope order is not significant. Omitted or exactly empty `resource` defaults to the verified GitHub Actions token's repository claim; Fly.io and Google callers must provide it explicitly. Omitted or exactly empty `scope` defaults to `contents:write pull_requests:write`. Whitespace-only, padded, duplicate, or multi-value `scope` and `resource` fields are rejected.
    40	
    41	Empty `scope` is not a no-permissions request. Following OAuth token endpoint parameter handling for this optional field, `scope=` is treated as omitted and receives the cyspbot default scope. GitHub's installation-token API treats an omitted `permissions` object as the app installation's default permissions, and live testing showed that a present empty `permissions: {}` object receives the same default permissions. cyspbot therefore requires a non-empty explicit scope when the caller does not want the cyspbot default.
    42	
    43	OAuth client authentication is not supported at `/token`. Requests with an `Authorization` header or non-empty client-authentication form parameters are rejected rather than silently ignored.
    44	
    45	Successful responses use OAuth token response shape with `Cache-Control: no-store` and `Pragma: no-cache`. The response always includes the canonical issued `scope`, so callers can observe defaults and normalized ordering:
    46	
    47	```json
    48	{
    49	  "access_token": "ghs_...",
    50	  "issued_token_type": "urn:chikachow:github-app-installation-access-token",
    51	  "token_type": "Bearer",
    52	  "scope": "contents:write pull_requests:write",
    53	  "expires_in": 3600
    54	}
    55	```
    56	
    57	### `POST /github/webhooks`
    58	
    59	Accepts signed JSON GitHub App webhook deliveries up to `256 KiB`. Webhook target headers must identify the configured GitHub App. Raw webhook bodies are not retained.
    60	
    61	Signed `ping` deliveries return `202 {"accepted":true,"event":"ping"}`. Any other valid signed JSON event returns `202 {"accepted":true}` with no event-specific parsing or downstream work.
    62	
    63	## Current Token Policy
    64	
    65	Installation Token Issuance is allowed only when a normalized token request matches an explicit checked-in Token Policy rule. Rules bind the verified subject-token issuer, exact resource and permissions, and a CEL condition over signed `claims`, `subject`, and normalized `request` data.
    66	
    67	- the caller is a verified GitHub Actions principal from the configured issuer
    68	- the signed subject token audience is `cyspbot`, and any `azp` claim is accepted only if it also matches `cyspbot`
    69	- `event_name` matches the checked-in rule
    70	- the OIDC subject context is `ref`
    71	- `ref` and the parsed subject ref exactly match the checked-in rule
    72	- the parsed subject repository name matches the signed `repository` claim, and immutable subject IDs are checked when GitHub includes them in `sub`
    73	- `workflow_ref` exactly matches the checked-in rule
    74	- `ref_type` is `branch`
    75	- the normalized token request `resource` and `permissions` exactly match the checked-in rule
    76	
    77	The caller cannot supply arbitrary GitHub Apps, GitHub permissions, or repository ids. The validated `scope` and validated `resource` are normalized into one installation token request, then policy answers whether the verified subject token may receive exactly that token. Cross-owner requests are possible only when explicitly allowed by policy. Unlisted repositories do not receive a default token.
    78	
    79	Repository identity in policy is intentionally based on GitHub owner/repository names rather than repository IDs. GitHub Actions OIDC tokens may carry repository IDs as separate signed claims, and immutable subject formats may repeat those IDs inside `sub`; the CEL condition requires the immutable `sub` IDs to agree with the corresponding signed claims, but policy matching itself remains name-based. A repository that is deleted and recreated with the same owner/name can match existing policy for that name, and token issuance still depends on the GitHub App being installed with sufficient permissions.
    80	
    81	The exact policy entries are intentionally not documented here. They are service-owned authorization data and may move from checked-in code to live configuration. The durable contract is deny-by-default: unlisted principal, resource, and permission combinations do not receive tokens.
    82	
    83	Fly Machine authentication does not create an implicit grant. A Fly rule must be added with `flyMachineInstallationTokenRule`, including the configured organization slug, signed immutable organization and app IDs, exact repository resource, and permissions. Use `machineId` when only one Machine should receive the grant.
    84	
    85	Google service-account authentication does not create an implicit grant. A Google rule must be added with `googleServiceAccountInstallationTokenRule`, including the immutable account unique ID, exact repository resource, and permissions. An optional email constraint is accepted only with `email_verified=true`.
    86	
    87	cyspbot denies forked pull request contexts, unconfigured refs, unconfigured workflow files, tag refs, unsupported event names, unsupported scopes, and non-canonical resource forms.
    88	
    89	## GitHub App Configuration
    90	
    91	The GitHub App registration is the upper-bound authorization control plane for repository permissions. cyspbot narrows issued tokens to one checked-in repository resource, allowed workflow context, and checked-in permission request.
    92	
    93	Webhook delivery requires the GitHub App webhook URL to point at:
    94	
    95	```text
    96	https://<your-cyspbot-origin>/github/webhooks
    97	```
    98	
    99	## Deployment Boundary
   100	
   101	The checked-in Wrangler configs are public-safe templates for local development, tests, and dry-runs. Production deployment is handled by a separate pipeline outside this codebase. See [docs/deployment.md](docs/deployment.md).
   102	
   103	Do not commit the downloaded key, converted key, `.dev.vars`, `.env`, `.wrangler/`, `.local-secrets/`, Cloudflare tokens, or deployment overlays.
   104	
   105	## Local Development
   106	
   107	1. Copy `.dev.vars.example` to `.dev.vars`.
   108	2. Fill in the GitHub App ID, webhook secret, and local PKCS#8 PEM private key.
   109	3. Install dependencies:
   110	
   111	   ```bash
   112	   pnpm install
   113	   ```
   114	
   115	4. Run checks:
   116	
   117	   ```bash
   118	   pnpm run check
   119	   ```
   120

72	- OIDC/JWKS provider unavailable: `503 {"error":"temporarily_unavailable"}`
    73	- unsupported target selector or policy denial: `400 {"error":"invalid_target"}`
    74	- upstream GitHub server failure: `502 {"error":"server_error"}`
    75	- internal server failure: `500 {"error":"server_error"}`
    76	
    77	OIDC/JWKS provider unavailability means cyspbot cannot obtain a usable trusted key set: JWKS network failures, timeouts, non-200 responses, malformed JSON, malformed shape, or ambiguous key matches. OIDC tokens whose JWT header names a `kid` absent from the usable JWKS are invalid subject tokens and return `400 {"error":"invalid_request"}` because the caller controls the `kid` header.
    78	
    79	### Token Policy
    80	
    81	Installation Token Issuance is allowed only when the normalized installation token request matches an explicit checked-in Token Policy rule. Each rule binds a verified subject-token issuer, exact resource and permissions, and a fail-closed CEL condition over signed `claims`, `subject`, and normalized `request` data:
    82	
    83	- the caller presents a verified [GitHub Actions OIDC](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/concepts/security/openid-connect) subject token from `https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com`
    84	- the signed subject token audience is `cyspbot`
    85	- if the OIDC token has an `azp` claim, that claim matches `cyspbot`
    86	- `event_name` is listed by the matching rule
    87	- `ref_type` is `branch`
    88	- `sub` is either the expected legacy repository/ref form or an immutable form consistent with the signed repository ID and, when present, owner ID claim
    89	- `repository`, `ref`, `sub`, and `workflow_ref` exactly satisfy the matching rule's CEL condition
    90	- normalized `resource` and `permissions` exactly match the matching rule
    91	
    92	Fly.io Machine tokens may authenticate only from organization issuers explicitly configured by the service. Authentication requires non-empty immutable organization, app, and Machine identity claims; `org_name` must match the configured issuer slug; and `sub` must equal `org_name:app_name:machine_name`. They remain denied unless an issuer-guarded Token Policy rule also matches immutable organization and app IDs, an optional Machine ID, the resource, and requested permissions.
    93	
    94	Google service-account ID tokens authenticate only from `https://accounts.google.com` and require matching non-empty `sub` and `azp` claims. They remain denied unless an issuer-guarded Token Policy rule also matches the immutable service-account unique ID, the resource, and requested permissions. A rule may additionally require the signed email only when `email_verified=true`.
    95	
    96	The caller cannot supply arbitrary GitHub Apps, GitHub permissions, or repository ids. The validated `scope` and validated `resource` are normalized into one installation token request. Token Policy answers whether the verified subject token may receive exactly that token request, including cross-owner requests when explicit policy allows them. cyspbot denies unconfigured issuer/condition/resource/permission combinations with `invalid_target`. The [GitHub App installation](https://docs.github.com/en/rest/apps/apps#create-an-installation-access-token-for-an-app) remains the upper-bound permission authority.
    97	
    98	Policy evaluates only facts present in the verified token. For the common legacy subject form, the rule requires `sub` to contain the same repository name as the signed `repository` claim; `repository_id` and `repository_owner_id` are not inputs to that authorization decision. For GitHub's immutable subject form, `repository_id` must be present and consistent with `sub`. When the optional `repository_owner_id` claim is present, it must also be a string consistent with `sub`; when it is absent or null, policy accepts any owner ID embedded in the otherwise matching immutable subject. These IDs check internal subject consistency but are not independent policy keys.
    99	
   100	Missing or incorrectly typed claims used by a configured policy condition authenticate as verified token data but fail policy with `400 {"error":"invalid_target"}`. Claims that the matching rule does not use, such as GitHub's `actor` metadata, do not affect authorization. Invalid issuer, signature, audience, expiry, `azp`, or subject binding remains an invalid subject token and returns `400 {"error":"invalid_request"}`. Subject matching is literal; percent-encoded repository or ref components are not decoded into an allowed subject.
   101	
   102	Token Policy intentionally uses GitHub owner/repository names as the externally meaningful repository identifier, even though [GitHub Actions OIDC](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/security/oidc) also exposes immutable repository and owner IDs and GitHub's installation-token API can scope by `repository_ids`. Those IDs participate in the immutable-subject consistency condition but are not independent policy keys. A repository that is deleted and recreated with the same owner/name can continue to match policy for that name when the GitHub App installation still grants sufficient permissions.
   103	
   104	For GitHub Actions only, omitted `scope` and `resource` produce this normalized permission request for cyspbot's service-owned GitHub App and the signed repository claim:
    78	
    79	- issuer: `https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com`
    80	- JWKS URI: `https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com/.well-known/jwks`
    81	- allowed signing algorithm: `RS256`
    82	
    83	Fly.io trusted issuers are derived from the comma-delimited `FLY_OIDC_ORG_SLUGS` Worker variable. Each valid organization slug maps to `https://oidc.fly.io/{org-slug}` and its issuer-relative `/.well-known/jwks` endpoint with `RS256`. Invalid configuration fails closed, and unconfigured Fly.io organization issuers are rejected before any JWKS fetch. Adapter sets are cached by the complete binding value so their trusted-issuer object identities remain stable and the shared verifier retains its remote JWKS cache across requests.
    84	
    85	The Fly adapter requires non-empty `org_id`, `org_name`, `app_id`, `app_name`, `machine_id`, `machine_name`, and `machine_version` claims. It binds `org_name` to the configured issuer slug and requires `sub` to equal `org_name:app_name:machine_name`. These checks establish a consistent authenticated Machine identity before policy evaluation.
    86	
    87	`workers/cyspbot-token-exchange/src/policy/fly-machine-token-policy-rule.ts` builds Fly allow rules using the configured issuer plus immutable organization and app IDs. A rule may also require one immutable Machine ID. Every rule still specifies an exact GitHub repository resource and permission map; there is no default Fly resource and no implicit production Fly grant.
    88	
    89	The Google service-account adapter trusts `https://accounts.google.com`, Google OAuth JWKS at `https://www.googleapis.com/oauth2/v3/certs`, and `RS256`. It requires non-empty `sub` and `azp` claims that exactly match. `workers/cyspbot-token-exchange/src/policy/google-service-account-token-policy-rule.ts` builds Google allow rules around the immutable service-account unique ID and can additionally require a signed email with `email_verified=true`. Every rule specifies an exact GitHub repository resource and permission map; there is no default Google resource and no implicit production Google grant.
    90	
    91	`packages/oidc/src/verifier.ts` verifies tokens with `jose.jwtVerify` and `jose.createRemoteJWKSet`. The generic verifier owns issuer, allowed-algorithm, signature, expiry, and JWKS checks. `packages/oidc/src/issuer-adapter.ts` defines how authentication recognizes a configured issuer and validates subject-token binding. Token-exchange authentication selects only from adapters composed in `workers/cyspbot-token-exchange/src/oidc-issuers.ts`; the unverified `iss` claim never supplies a JWKS URI. The GitHub Actions adapter owns the exact match between any signed optional authorized-party claim and cyspbot's internal service audience, while authentication validates the signed `aud` claim.

    93	OIDC/JWKS failures are classified at the verifier boundary:

Message Type: FINAL_ANSWER
Task name: /root
Sender: /root/spec_review
Payload:
1. **P2 — Compatible-token acquisition is undocumented.** [README.md](/Users/cysp/src/cyspbot/README.md:37) requires `aud=cyspbot`, and [README.md](/Users/cysp/src/cyspbot/README.md:85) allows email-constrained rules, but callers are never told to request a service-account token with `audience: "cyspbot"` and `includeEmail: true` when needed. Google says `audience` is required and only `includeEmail=true` adds `email` and `email_verified`. See [IAM Credentials API](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/reference/credentials/rest/v1/projects.serviceAccounts/generateIdToken) and [Get an ID token](https://docs.cloud.google.com/docs/authentication/get-id-token).

2. **P3 — Service-contract requirements contradict Google support.** [docs/service-contract.md](/Users/cysp/src/cyspbot/docs/service-contract.md:81) says each issuance requires the following conditions, but lines 83–90 require GitHub Actions and GitHub-only claims (`event_name`, `ref_type`, `repository`, `ref`, `workflow_ref`). Google tokens cannot satisfy these, while line 94 says they are supported. Those bullets should be explicitly GitHub-specific.

No implemented-but-wrong Google runtime behavior or Google-specific scope creep found. [issuer.ts](/Users/cysp/src/cyspbot/packages/oidc-issuer-google-service-account/src/issuer.ts:4) matches Google’s current [service-account ID-token contract](https://docs.cloud.google.com/docs/authentication/token-types#service_account_id_tokens): RS256/global Google JWKS, exact HTTPS issuer, requester-chosen audience, and service-account unique ID in matching `azp`/`sub`. Audience is checked centrally in [authentication.ts](/Users/cysp/src/cyspbot/workers/cyspbot-token-exchange/src/authentication.ts:85), and email policy correctly requires `email_verified=true`.

payload.message:
The Google OIDC runtime implementation is sound against current Google guidance. I found no authentication bypass, issuer-confusion path, or incorrect Google claim validation. I did find three documentation problems and two lower-confidence design smells.

## Standards

1. **Documentation contradiction — hard violation**

   [CONTEXT.md](/Users/cysp/src/cyspbot/CONTEXT.md:18) says cyspbot does not require issuer-specific claims unless policy names them. That is no longer true: the Google adapter requires `sub` and `azp`, while Fly requires several identity claims before policy evaluation.

   This conflicts with the provider-binding behavior documented immediately below and violates the requirement in [CONTRIBUTING.md](/Users/cysp/src/cyspbot/CONTRIBUTING.md:6) to update trust-boundary terminology. The statement should distinguish adapter-level identity requirements from policy-selected claims.

2. **Duplicated binding conditions — judgment call**

   Google repeats `azp == sub` in both [issuer.ts](/Users/cysp/src/cyspbot/packages/oidc-issuer-google-service-account/src/issuer.ts:15) and [google-service-account-token-policy-rule.ts](/Users/cysp/src/cyspbot/workers/cyspbot-token-exchange/src/policy/google-service-account-token-policy-rule.ts:30). Fly similarly repeats its canonical-subject conditions in authentication and policy.

   This is fail-closed and not currently incorrect, but it creates two definitions that could drift. Provider identity consistency belongs naturally in the adapter; policy should generally select which authenticated identity receives a grant.

3. **Raw issuer interface — judgment call**

   [issuer-adapter.ts](/Users/cysp/src/cyspbot/packages/oidc/src/issuer-adapter.ts:8) passes `issuer: string` into every provider-binding validator. Fly needs it, Google redundantly rechecks it, and GitHub ignores it. Passing the already resolved trusted issuer or a provider-specific binding context would express the trust boundary more clearly.

## Spec and Google guidance

1. **P2 — Callers are not shown how to obtain a compatible token**

   [README.md](/Users/cysp/src/cyspbot/README.md:37) requires `aud=cyspbot`, and line 85 permits email-constrained rules, but there is no usage example telling Google callers to request:

   - `audience: "cyspbot"`
   - `includeEmail: true` when the configured rule checks email

   Google documents `audience` as required and says that `email` and `email_verified` are included only when `includeEmail=true`. See the [IAM Credentials `generateIdToken` reference](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/reference/credentials/rest/v1/projects.serviceAccounts/generateIdToken) and [Google’s ID-token acquisition guide](https://docs.cloud.google.com/docs/authentication/get-id-token).

2. **P3 — The service contract presents GitHub-only requirements as universal**

   [docs/service-contract.md](/Users/cysp/src/cyspbot/docs/service-contract.md:81) introduces the following bullets as requirements for “each rule,” but lines 83–90 require GitHub Actions and GitHub-only claims such as `event_name`, `ref_type`, `repository`, and `workflow_ref`. Line 94 then says Google service accounts are supported.

   Those bullets should be labeled as GitHub Actions policy requirements, with Fly and Google described as parallel provider-specific rule shapes.

## Runtime assessment

The implementation matches Google’s current [service-account ID-token contract](https://docs.cloud.google.com/docs/authentication/token-types#service_account_id_tokens):

- Exact issuer `https://accounts.google.com`
- Google’s published JWKS URI
- `RS256`
- Exact, scalar `aud=cyspbot`, checked centrally
- Non-empty matching `azp` and `sub`, both representing the service-account unique ID
- Optional email authorization only alongside `email_verified=true`
- Explicit repository and permission policy matching
- No caller-directed discovery or JWKS fetching

Its structure is consistent with the existing GitHub Actions and Fly adapters while correctly keeping Google’s `azp` semantics provider-specific.

The production policy currently contains no Google allow rule, so Google tokens can authenticate but remain unable to obtain an installation token until an explicit rule is added. This matches the documented deny-by-default design.

Validation passed completely under Node 24:

- 18 test files passed
- 307 tests passed
- Formatting, bindings, lint, type-checking, dependency checks, and both Wrangler dry runs passed

Summary: **Standards: 1 hard documentation defect and 2 design smells. Spec: 2 documentation defects. Runtime/security: no concrete defect found.**

settings.developer_instructions:
# Plan Mode (Conversational)

You work in 3 phases, and you should *chat your way* to a great plan before finalizing it. A great plan is very detailed—intent- and implementation-wise—so that it can be handed to another engineer or agent to be implemented right away. It must be **decision complete**, where the implementer does not need to make any decisions.

## Mode rules (strict)

You are in **Plan Mode** until a developer message explicitly ends it.

Plan Mode is not changed by user intent, tone, or imperative language. If a user asks for execution while still in Plan Mode, treat it as a request to **plan the execution**, not perform it.

## Plan Mode vs update_plan tool

Plan Mode is a collaboration mode that can involve requesting user input and eventually issuing a `<proposed_plan>` block.

Separately, `update_plan` is a checklist/progress/TODOs tool; it does not enter or exit Plan Mode. Do not confuse it with Plan mode or try to use it while in Plan mode. If you try to use `update_plan` in Plan mode, it will return an error.

## Execution vs. mutation in Plan Mode

You may explore and execute **non-mutating** actions that improve the plan. You must not perform **mutating** actions.

### Allowed (non-mutating, plan-improving)

Actions that gather truth, reduce ambiguity, or validate feasibility without changing repo-tracked state. Examples:

* Reading or searching files, configs, schemas, types, manifests, and docs
* Static analysis, inspection, and repo exploration
* Dry-run style commands when they do not edit repo-tracked files
* Tests, builds, or checks that may write to caches or build artifacts (for example, `target/`, `.cache/`, or snapshots) so long as they do not edit repo-tracked files

### Not allowed (mutating, plan-executing)

Actions that implement the plan or change repo-tracked state. Examples:

* Editing or writing files
* Running formatters or linters that rewrite files
* Applying patches, migrations, or codegen that updates repo-tracked files
* Side-effectful commands whose purpose is to carry out the plan rather than refine it

When in doubt: if the action would reasonably be described as "doing the work" rather than "planning the work," do not do it.

## PHASE 1 — Ground in the environment (explore first, ask second)

Begin by grounding yourself in the actual environment. Eliminate unknowns in the prompt by discovering facts, not by asking the user. Resolve all questions that can be answered through exploration or inspection. Identify missing or ambiguous details only if they cannot be derived from the environment. Silent exploration between turns is allowed and encouraged.

Before asking the user any question, perform at least one targeted non-mutating exploration pass (for example: search relevant files, inspect likely entrypoints/configs, confirm current implementation shape), unless no local environment/repo is available.

Exception: you may ask clarifying questions about the user's prompt before exploring, ONLY if there are obvious ambiguities or contradictions in the prompt itself. However, if ambiguity might be resolved by exploring, always prefer exploring first.

Do not ask questions that can be answered from the repo or system (for example, "where is this struct?" or "which UI component should we use?" when exploration can make it clear). Only ask once you have exhausted reasonable non-mutating exploration.

## PHASE 2 — Intent chat (what they actually want)

* Keep asking until you can clearly state: goal + success criteria, audience, in/out of scope, constraints, current state, and the key preferences/tradeoffs.
* Bias toward questions over guessing: if any high-impact ambiguity remains, do NOT plan yet—ask.

## PHASE 3 — Implementation chat (what/how we’ll build)

* Once intent is stable, keep asking until the spec is decision complete: approach, interfaces (APIs/schemas/I/O), data flow, edge cases/failure modes, testing + acceptance criteria, rollout/monitoring, and any migrations/compat constraints.

## Asking questions

Critical rules:

* Strongly prefer using the `request_user_input` tool to ask any questions.
* Offer only meaningful multiple‑choice options; don’t include filler choices that are obviously wrong or irrelevant.
* In rare cases where an unavoidable, important question can’t be expressed with reasonable multiple‑choice options (due to extreme ambiguity), you may ask it directly without the tool.

You SHOULD ask many questions, but each question must:

* materially change the spec/plan, OR
* confirm/lock an assumption, OR
* choose between meaningful tradeoffs.
* not be answerable by non-mutating commands.

Use the `request_user_input` tool only for decisions that materially change the plan, for confirming important assumptions, or for information that cannot be discovered via non-mutating exploration.

## Two kinds of unknowns (treat differently)

1. **Discoverable facts** (repo/system truth): explore first.

   * Before asking, run targeted searches and check likely sources of truth (configs/manifests/entrypoints/schemas/types/constants).
   * Ask only if: multiple plausible candidates; nothing found but you need a missing identifier/context; or ambiguity is actually product intent.
   * If asking, present concrete candidates (paths/service names) + recommend one.
   * Never ask questions you can answer from your environment (e.g., “where is this struct”).

2. **Preferences/tradeoffs** (not discoverable): ask early.

   * These are intent or implementation preferences that cannot be derived from exploration.
   * Provide 2–4 mutually exclusive options + a recommended default.
   * If unanswered, proceed with the recommended option and record it as an assumption in the final plan.

## Finalization rule

Only output the final plan when it is decision complete and leaves no decisions to the implementer.

When you present the official plan, wrap it in a `<proposed_plan>` block so the client can render it specially:

1) The opening tag must be on its own line.
2) Start the plan content on the next line (no text on the same line as the tag).
3) The closing tag must be on its own line.
4) Use Markdown inside the block.
5) Keep the tags exactly as `<proposed_plan>` and `</proposed_plan>` (do not translate or rename them), even if the plan content is in another language.

Example:

<proposed_plan>
plan content
</proposed_plan>

plan content should be human and agent digestible. The final plan must be plan-only, concise by default, and include:

* A clear title
* A brief summary section
* Important changes or additions to public APIs/interfaces/types
* Test cases and scenarios
* Explicit assumptions and defaults chosen where needed

When possible, prefer a compact structure with 3-5 short sections, usually: Summary, Key Changes or Implementation Changes, Test Plan, and Assumptions. Do not include a separate Scope section unless scope boundaries are genuinely important to avoid mistakes.

Prefer grouped implementation bullets by subsystem or behavior over file-by-file inventories. Mention files only when needed to disambiguate a non-obvious change, and avoid naming more than 3 paths unless extra specificity is necessary to prevent mistakes. Prefer behavior-level descriptions over symbol-by-symbol removal lists. For v1 feature-addition plans, do not invent detailed schema, validation, precedence, fallback, or wire-shape policy unless the request establishes it or it is needed to prevent a concrete implementation mistake; prefer the intended capability and minimum interface/behavior changes.

Keep bullets short and avoid explanatory sub-bullets unless they are needed to prevent ambiguity. Prefer the minimum detail needed for implementation safety, not exhaustive coverage. Within each section, compress related changes into a few high-signal bullets and omit branch-by-branch logic, repeated invariants, and long lists of unaffected behavior unless they are necessary to prevent a likely implementation mistake. Avoid repeated repo facts and irrelevant edge-case or rollout detail. For straightforward refactors, keep the plan to a compact summary, key edits, tests, and assumptions. If the user asks for more detail, then expand.

Do not ask "should I proceed?" in the final output. The user can easily switch out of Plan mode and request implementation if you have included a `<proposed_plan>` block in your response. Alternatively, they can decide to stay in Plan mode and continue refining the plan.

Only produce at most one `<proposed_plan>` block per turn, and only when you are presenting a complete spec.

If the user stays in Plan mode and asks for revisions after a prior `<proposed_plan>`, any new `<proposed_plan>` must be a complete replacement. If the user indicates that the prior plan is not acceptable but does not provide enough information to produce a complete replacement, address the concern and continue planning without producing a `<proposed_plan>` block. If the follow-up neither requires changes nor calls the plan into question (e.g. clarifying question), answer it before the block, then reproduce the prior `<proposed_plan>` unchanged.

item.text:
# Address Google OIDC Review Findings

## Summary

Correct the documentation contradictions, add actionable Google token-acquisition guidance, and tighten the issuer-adapter/policy separation without changing the public `/token` wire contract or authorization outcomes.

The refactor will preserve these invariants:

- Google authentication continues to require Google-signed `RS256` ID tokens from `https://accounts.google.com`.
- `aud` remains the exact scalar value `cyspbot`.
- Google service-account identity remains the non-empty unique ID represented by matching `sub` and `azp`.
- Email remains optional and usable for authorization only when `email_verified=true`.
- Google and Fly callers must provide an explicit repository resource.
- Authentication alone never grants a GitHub token; checked-in policy remains deny-by-default.
- Existing production policy remains unchanged and therefore continues to contain no implicit Google or Fly grant.

## Implementation Changes

### 1. Correct the domain and service-contract documentation

- Revise the **Subject Token Claims** definition in `CONTEXT.md` to distinguish:
  - provider-binding claims required by an issuer adapter to establish an internally consistent identity; and
  - additional verified claims named by Token Policy to select an authorization grant.
- State positively that issuer adapters may require provider-specific claims before policy evaluation.
- State negatively that an adapter requirement is not itself an authorization grant and that policy does not evaluate raw or unverified claims.
- Preserve the existing Google and Fly identity definitions as concrete examples of adapter-level binding.

- Restructure the Token Policy section in `docs/service-contract.md`:
  - Keep the universal requirements limited to verified issuer, exact resource, exact permissions, and a fail-closed CEL condition.
  - Introduce the existing GitHub claim list explicitly as “GitHub Actions policy rules require…”.
  - Keep Fly and Google requirements in parallel provider-specific paragraphs.
  - Clarify that Google rules do not require GitHub Actions claims such as `repository`, `event_name`, `ref`, or `workflow_ref`.
  - Avoid implying that every issuer uses the same claim vocabulary.

### 2. Add usable Google token-acquisition guidance

- Add a concise Google service-account example to the repository README near the `/token` usage documentation.
- Use the IAM Credentials API’s `projects.serviceAccounts.generateIdToken` flow with:
  - `audience: "cyspbot"`;
  - `includeEmail: false` for rules based only on the immutable unique ID;
  - `includeEmail: true` only when the configured policy rule also checks the service-account email.
- Show that the returned JWT is supplied as the RFC 8693 `subject_token`, with:
  - `subject_token_type=urn:ietf:params:oauth:token-type:id_token`;
  - an explicit canonical GitHub API repository `resource`;
  - the desired supported permission `scope`.
- Explain that `audience` in Google’s token-generation request sets the ID token’s signed `aud` claim and is distinct from cyspbot’s unsupported RFC 8693 `audience` form parameter.
- Link directly to Google’s authoritative `generateIdToken` and ID-token acquisition documentation.
- Note that service-account impersonation requires the appropriate Google IAM permission and should use short-lived Google-issued credentials rather than downloaded service-account keys.
- Do not document secrets, deployment-specific service-account identities, production repository grants, or a real bearer token.
- Do not recommend the `tokeninfo` debugging endpoint for production validation; cyspbot already performs local signature and claim validation against Google’s JWKS.

### 3. Make provider binding the single source of identity consistency

- Keep `azp == sub` as a Google authentication invariant in the Google issuer adapter. This is required to distinguish the accepted service-account token shape before policy evaluation.
- Remove the redundant `claims["azp"] == claims["sub"]` expression from generated Google policy conditions.
- Google policy conditions should select grants using:
  - exact `sub == configured uniqueId`;
  - optional exact email plus `email_verified == true`;
  - the existing exact issuer, resource, and permission matching outside the CEL expression.
- Do not move `azp/sub` validation entirely into policy: doing so would allow tokens outside the declared Google service-account identity profile to become authenticated contexts.

- Apply the same separation to Fly:
  - retain issuer-slug, canonical-subject, and required identity-claim consistency checks in the Fly adapter;
  - remove duplicate issuer/name and canonical-subject consistency expressions from the Fly policy builder;
  - retain immutable organization ID, app ID, and optional Machine ID as policy grant selectors.
- Do not weaken fail-closed behavior for missing or incorrectly typed claims.
- Document in the implementation reference that adapters establish provider identity consistency while policy builders select which authenticated identities receive which grants.

### 4. Strengthen the issuer-adapter interface

- Replace the raw `issuer: string` binding-validator input with the resolved `TrustedOidcIssuer`.
- Authentication must pass the same trusted-issuer object selected by `resolveIssuer`, not reconstruct one from token claims.
- Fly must derive its issuer slug from `trustedIssuer.issuer`.
- Google should stop redundantly comparing the issuer during binding validation because successful resolution and central JWT verification already establish it.
- GitHub Actions may continue to ignore the resolved issuer because its provider-specific binding only depends on `sub`, `exp`, `azp`, and the expected audience.
- Name the field `trustedIssuer` to make its trust status explicit.
- Do not pass the original unverified `iss` string into the post-verification binding interface.
- Do not introduce dynamic discovery, provider registries, generic claim maps, subclasses, or a larger provider framework; keep this a narrow contract clarification.

## Public Interfaces and Compatibility

- `/token`, its form parameters, response shape, error mapping, supported scopes, and resource normalization remain unchanged.
- `OidcIssuerAdapter.validateSubjectTokenBinding` changes internally from receiving `issuer: string` to `trustedIssuer: TrustedOidcIssuer`.
- Generated Google and Fly CEL policy condition strings will become narrower, but effective authorization must remain unchanged because the removed conditions are already mandatory adapter invariants.
- Existing policy-builder call signatures remain unchanged.
- Existing checked-in policy entries remain unchanged; do not add a sample Google rule to production policy.
- Documentation examples must use placeholders and must not imply that Google callers are currently authorized in the deployed service.

## Test Plan

### Adapter contract tests

- Google accepts a verified token only when:
  - `sub` is a non-empty string;
  - `azp` is a non-empty string equal to `sub`;
  - `exp` is numeric;
  - the resolved trusted issuer is Google’s configured issuer.
- Add explicit negative cases for missing, empty, incorrectly typed, and mismatched `sub`/`azp`.
- Confirm an unhandled Google-like issuer is rejected before JWKS fetching.
- Confirm incorrect audience, plural audience, invalid signature, expired token, and unsupported algorithm continue to fail centrally.

- Update Fly tests to pass `trustedIssuer` and confirm canonical identity checks remain in the adapter after their policy duplicates are removed.
- Update GitHub Actions tests for the interface change and confirm its existing `azp` behavior is unchanged.

### Policy tests

- Assert the Google policy condition contains the exact unique-ID selector.
- For email-constrained rules, assert it requires both exact email and `email_verified == true`.
- Assert the generated condition no longer contains `azp == sub`.
- Prove behavior remains fail-closed for:
  - wrong unique ID;
  - missing or false `email_verified`;
  - wrong email;
  - wrong issuer;
  - wrong resource;
  - wrong permissions.
- Add an authentication-plus-policy integration case showing that an `azp/sub` mismatch is rejected during authentication and therefore never reaches policy.

- Assert Fly policy no longer duplicates adapter-owned canonical identity conditions while still denying wrong organization ID, app ID, optional Machine ID, resource, or permissions.

### Documentation and repository validation

- Extend documentation tests, if their current structure permits, to verify that provider-specific Token Policy sections do not present GitHub-only claims as universal requirements.
- Ensure documented example parameter names and URNs match the actual parser constants.
- Run the complete Node 24 validation path:
  - frozen lockfile check;
  - formatting check;
  - generated binding check;
  - lint;
  - TypeScript typecheck;
  - unused-code/dependency check;
  - all tests;
  - both Wrangler dry-run checks.
- Acceptance requires the existing 307 tests plus the new negative and integration cases to pass with no snapshots or production policy grants changed.

## Positive and Negative Engineering Guidance

- Prefer explicit trust-boundary language: verification establishes authenticity, provider binding establishes identity consistency, and Token Policy authorizes a concrete grant.
- Keep immutable Google `sub` as the primary configured identifier; treat email only as an optional secondary constraint.
- Keep all issuer and JWKS endpoints service-owned and exact.
- Keep examples operational enough to copy while using placeholders for identities and repositories.

- Do not accept Google user ID tokens, IAP assertions, unsigned JWTs, access tokens, plural audiences, or alternate issuers as part of this change.
- Do not accept `accounts.google.com` without the HTTPS scheme; Google’s current service-account token documentation says this token type always uses `https://accounts.google.com`.
- Do not make email mandatory for service-account identity.
- Do not use email as the sole policy identity.
- Do not infer a repository from Google claims or add a default Google resource.
- Do not add live Google/Fly production grants, deployment configuration, secrets, or service-account keys.
- Do not remove provider-binding checks merely because policy currently repeats them.
- Do not broaden the refactor into a new policy language or dynamic issuer architecture.

## Assumptions

- All five reported items are in scope: three documentation corrections and both design-smell improvements.
- Google support targets Google service-account ID tokens described by the current Google Cloud token contract, not general Sign in with Google user tokens.
- The IAM Credentials API is the primary documented acquisition path because it exposes both required `audience` and optional `includeEmail` controls.
- Runtime authorization behavior must remain identical; this work clarifies ownership of invariants and improves usability rather than expanding access.
